sunday.themeNote: This Sunday's readings paint a picture of a God who is gentle, close, and full of compassion — not distant or demanding. The prophet Zechariah foretold a king who would come humbly, on a donkey, bringing peace rather than war, and Jesus fulfills that promise perfectly in the Gospel. Together, the readings invite us to stop straining under life's weight and simply come to Jesus, trusting that his way is easier than anything we carry alone.

readings.label: First Reading
readings.fullText:
Thus says the LORD:
Rejoice heartily, O daughter Zion,
shout for joy, O daughter Jerusalem!
See, your king shall come to you;
a just savior is he,
meek, and riding on an ass,
on a colt, the foal of an ass.
He shall banish the chariot from Ephraim,
and the horse from Jerusalem;
the warrior's bow shall be banished,
and he shall proclaim peace to the nations.
His dominion shall be from sea to sea,
and from the River to the ends of the earth.
readings.summary: About 500 years before Jesus, the prophet Zechariah gave the people of Jerusalem a surprising message: their coming king would arrive not on a warhorse, but on a humble donkey. This king would not conquer through violence — he would bring peace to the whole world. It's a vision that points straight to Jesus, who entered Jerusalem on Palm Sunday in exactly this way. God's power, it turns out, looks very different from what the world expects.

readings.label: Responsorial Psalm
readings.fullText:
R. (cf. 1) I will praise your name for ever, my king and my God.
or:
R. Alleluia.
I will extol you, O my God and King,
and I will bless your name forever and ever.
Every day will I bless you,
and I will praise your name forever and ever.
R. I will praise your name for ever, my king and my God.
or:
R. Alleluia.
The LORD is gracious and merciful,
slow to anger and of great kindness.
The LORD is good to all
and compassionate toward all his works.
R. I will praise your name for ever, my king and my God.
or:
R. Alleluia.
Let all your works give you thanks, O LORD,
and let your faithful ones bless you.
Let them discourse of the glory of your kingdom
and speak of your might.
R. I will praise your name for ever, my king and my God.
or:
R.  Alleluia.
The LORD is faithful in all his words
and holy in all his works.
The LORD lifts up all who are falling
and raises up all who are bowed down.
R. I will praise your name for ever, my king and my God.
or:
R. Alleluia.
readings.summary: Psalm 145 is a song of praise that celebrates who God truly is: gracious, merciful, slow to anger, and overflowing with kindness. The psalmist isn't just praising God out of duty — he's moved by genuine wonder at how good God is to everyone, even those who are struggling or falling. Praying this psalm at Mass is our way of joining that same chorus of gratitude. It reminds us that the God we are coming to know is not harsh or indifferent, but deeply caring.

readings.label: Second Reading
readings.fullText:
Brothers and sisters:
You are not in the flesh;
on the contrary, you are in the spirit,
if only the Spirit of God dwells in you.
Whoever does not have the Spirit of Christ does not belong to him.
If the Spirit of the one who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you,
the one who raised Christ from the dead
will give life to your mortal bodies also,
through his Spirit that dwells in you.
Consequently, brothers and sisters,
we are not debtors to the flesh,
to live according to the flesh.
For if you live according to the flesh, you will die,
but if by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body,
you will live.
readings.summary: Saint Paul writes to the early Christians in Rome about the difference between living by the flesh and living by the Spirit. Living 'by the flesh' means letting our worst impulses and selfish desires run our lives — a path that leads away from real life. But when God's Spirit lives in us, the same power that raised Jesus from the dead is at work within us, giving us the strength to choose better and truly live. This is one of the great gifts of baptism: we are never left to manage on our own.

readings.label: Gospel
readings.fullText:
At that time Jesus exclaimed:
"I give praise to you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth,
for although you have hidden these things
from the wise and the learned
you have revealed them to little ones.
Yes, Father, such has been your gracious will.
All things have been handed over to me by my Father.
No one knows the Son except the Father,
and no one knows the Father except the Son
and anyone to whom the Son wishes to reveal him."

"Come to me, all you who labor and are burdened,
and I will give you rest.
Take my yoke upon you and learn from me,
for I am meek and humble of heart;
and you will find rest for yourselves.
For my yoke is easy, and my burden light."
readings.summary: In the Gospel, Jesus offers one of the most comforting invitations in all of Scripture: 'Come to me, all you who labor and are burdened, and I will give you rest.' He isn't promising a life without difficulty, but he is promising that we don't have to carry our burdens alone. Jesus describes himself as meek and humble of heart — the same gentle king Zechariah foretold — and he asks us simply to learn from him and walk with him. His 'yoke' is the image of two oxen sharing a load together; Jesus is right beside us, making the weight lighter.
